coredata.dc:title: Deformation effect on graphene quantum dot/graphane and silicene quantum dot/silicane array
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Bi-Ru
coredata.dc:description: This article presents a design for the two-dimensional heterostructure systems (2DHS) consisting of graphene quantum dot (QD) array in graphane (GQD/Graphane) and silicene QD array in silicane (SiQD/Silicane). First-principles method was used to evaluate the effect of deformation on the magnetism and electronic properties of these 2DHS. For the band engineering, the tuning range of the energy gap of GQD/Graphane and SiQD/Silicane 2DHS can be up to 1.20 and 1.35 eV, respectively, through strains ranging from −7% to 10 %. A strain-sharing effect is found, wherein the hydrogenated region shares a portion of strain within the QD array. This effect is stronger in SiQD/silicane than in GQD/graphane. Strain sharing enhances band coupling between the QDs and their hydrogenated counterpart in the low-energy region. This alters the electronic properties of the 2DHS and the magnetic properties of triangular and parallelogram SiQD/Silicane arrays under large compressive strain. Strain modulates the band gap of 2DHS, triggers a phase transition from semiconductor to metal in SiQD/Silicane systems under homogeneous strain, and removes the magnetism of triangular and parallelogram SiQD arrays under compressive strain. These findings suggest that the 2DHS could be promising for designing nanoelectronic devices and binary logic based on nanoscale magnetism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2D heterostructure system
Quantum dots
Band engineering
Strain
Phase transition

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Strain effect on electronics of GQDs/Graphane
Strain effect on electronics of SiQDs/Silicane

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Cheng Gung University s under grant number UMRPD5M001.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Top view of a Hex-24 QD, a Tri-16 QD and side view of GQD and SiQD, where the edges are saturated by hydrogen atoms. (b) The 4 × 4 Hex-6 QD array, where D represents the inter-dot distance. (c) The system is under H- (left), A- (middle), and Z- (right) strains. The first Brillouin zone are also shown in the right lower side respected to the strains.

Strain energies of Hex-6 QDs for (a) GQD/Graphane and (b) Hex-6 SiQD/Silicane 2DHS under H-strains with various periodic conditions. The insets display the strain energy of graphane and graphene (silicane and silicene). The inter-dot interaction energies of Hex-6 and Tri-4 QD arrays for (c) GQD/Graphane and (d) SiQD/Silicane 2DHS, with the energy in a 12 × 12 periodic condition serving as the reference for the system.

(a) Total magnetic moments of Tri-4 SiQDs in various periodic conditions under strains. (b) Total magnetic moments of triangular SiQDs with N = 4, 16, 25, 36 in a 6 × 6 periodic condition under strains. Units are in Bohr magneton
μ B
.

Energy gap of Hex-6 and Tri4 GQD/Graphane 2DHS with four periodic conditions under (a) H-, (b) A-, and (c) Z-strain. The circles and triangles denote the energy gap of the Hex-6 and Tri4 GQD/Graphane 2DHS, respectively. The solid circles and triangles are energy gaps of single Hex-6 and Tri-4 GQDs, respectively. The magenta dashed line represents the energy gap of graphane.

Variations for energy gap of GQD/Graphene 2DHS with three types of strain in a 6x6 periodic condition. Panels (a), (b) and (c) correspond to H-, A-, and Z-strains, respectively. The energy gap values for single Hex-24, Tri-36, and Par-72 GQDs are denoted by black solid circles, solid triangles, and solid diamonds, respectively.

Band structures of GQD/Graphane 2DHS under H-strains for two distinct lattice geometries: (a) Hex-24 6 × 6 GQD arrays and (b) Tri-36 8 × 8 GQD arrays. In panel (b), magenta lines represent the majority spin, while the blue lines represent the minority spin.

Energy gap of Hex-6 and Tri-4 SiQD/Silicane 2DHS with six periodic conditions under (a) H-, (b) A-, and (c) Z-strain. The strain-E g curves of Par-8 SiQD 2DHS under (d) H-, (e) A-, and (f) Z-strain. Black dashed lines are the E g of silicane under strains.

Energy gap of SiQD/Silicane under H-, A-, and Z-strains. The hexagonal and triangular SiQD/Silicane are in a 6 × 6 periodic condition, (a–c) for hexagonal dots and (d–f) triangular dots, as well as for parallelogram-shaped SiQD arrays in an 8 × 8 periodic condition (g–i). The black solid circles, blue solid triangles, and solid diamonds denote the energy gap of a single Hex-24, Tri-36, and Par-32, Par-72 SiQD, respectively.

Band structures of SiQD/Silicane 2DHS in 6 × 6 periodic condition under H-strains. The considered lattice geometries include (a) Hex-24, (b) Tri-36, and (c) Par-32. Magenta and blue lines represent majority and minority spins, respectively, while black solid lines denote the nonmagnetic bands.

head.ce:title: Deformation effect on graphene quantum dot/graphane and silicene quantum dot/silicane array

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Natural Science, Center for General Education, Chang Gung University, No.259, Wenhua 1st Rd., Guishan Dist., Taoyuan City, 33302, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Natural Science, Center for General Education, Chang Gung University, No.259, Wenhua 1st Rd., Guishan Dist., Taoyuan City, 33302, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article presents a design for the two-dimensional heterostructure systems (2DHS) consisting of graphene quantum dot (QD) array in graphane (GQD/Graphane) and silicene QD array in silicane (SiQD/Silicane). First-principles method was used to evaluate the effect of deformation on the magnetism and electronic properties of these 2DHS. For the band engineering, the tuning range of the energy gap of GQD/Graphane and SiQD/Silicane 2DHS can be up to 1.20 and 1.35 eV, respectively, through strains ranging from −7% to 10 %. A strain-sharing effect is found, wherein the hydrogenated region shares a portion of strain within the QD array. This effect is stronger in SiQD/silicane than in GQD/graphane. Strain sharing enhances band coupling between the QDs and their hydrogenated counterpart in the low-energy region. This alters the electronic properties of the 2DHS and the magnetic properties of triangular and parallelogram SiQD/Silicane arrays under large compressive strain. Strain modulates the band gap of 2DHS, triggers a phase transition from semiconductor to metal in SiQD/Silicane systems under homogeneous strain, and removes the magnetism of triangular and parallelogram SiQD arrays under compressive strain. These findings suggest that the 2DHS could be promising for designing nanoelectronic devices and binary logic based on nanoscale magnetism.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have been extensively investigated and possess incredible properties that are distinct from their bulk structures [1,2]. These unique properties are primarily attributed to the large surface area and quantum confinement effect of nanomaterials. Significant progress in the synthesis technology of two-dimensional (2D) materials has opened a new research frontier for scientists. In particular, the heterostructure of 2D materials with different layers or in the same layer has garnered considerable attention [3–7]. 2D quantum dots (QDs) are zero-dimensional structures, and the movement of charge carriers in 2D QDs is more restricted compared to traditional three-dimensional QDs, resulting in a stronger quantum confinement effect. This offers an opportunity to explore the quantum confinement effect in different dimensions. 2D QDs also retain the character of traditional QDs, such as flexibility and easy tuning of electronic and optical properties [8–14]. Moreover, they can be easily designed by varying different sizes and shapes, which determine their electronic and magnetic properties [15,16]. The flexibility in designed 2D QDs allows for wide applications, such as in optical devices, electronic devices, biosensing, cancer therapy, and quantum computing [12–24].

Graphene, the first synthesized 2D material, has sparked intense research interest in 2D materials. It possesses many superior properties and behaves as a semimetal, but its zero bandgap limits its applications. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional flat structures that can be cut from graphene. Due to strong quantum confinements, GQDs overcome the weak point of graphene and exhibit an energy gap. They possess additional tunable properties compared to graphene, such as size, shape, dispersibility, and more active adsorption sites [15,19,20]. Silicene, the group IV cousin of graphene, has a similar honeycomb structure to graphene, but the silicon atoms are buckled instead of flat, as in graphene. Traditionally, silicon is the primary material used for electronic devices, and the use of silicene in nanoelectronics is expected to reduce the application gap and industry cost. Research on nano-structured silicene has been extensive [25–27], and the first silicene transistor was achieved in 2015 [28]. Silicene quantum dots (SiQDs), like GQDs, have great potential for a wide range of applications, such as in nano-optical devices, light-emitting diodes, flexible displays, biosensing, bioimaging, therapeutics, drug delivery, photovoltaics, and catalysis [29–32]. Recently, the properties of combined nanomaterials have been explored for their heterojunction effects, such as GQDs on one-dimensional TiO2 or 2D MoS2 to enhance the hydrogen evolution reaction [33,34]. GQDs on boron nitride nanosheets for microwave absorption [35], and SiQDs in few-layer siloxane for UV–visible absorption and photoluminescence emission [31]. Hydrogenation of graphene and silicene is a widely studied topic, including the band gap, electronic, and magnetic properties of the one-side, partially, and fully two-side hydrogenated graphene and silicene [36–41]. Additionally, researchers have explored incorporating embedded GQDs in graphane or SiQDs in silicane for the heterojunction of GQDs and graphane or SiQDs and silicane [15,16].

As quantum confinement has a large influence on 2D QDs, the size of 2D QDs plays a key role in their band gap [42]. The band gap can be tuned by varying the size of 2D QDs, and it is reduced as the size of the QDs grows larger. In addition to size, the shape of the 2D QDs is another important factor that affects the band gap. For both GQDs and SiQDs, the hexagonal shape with zigzag edges has the largest band gap, while the triangular shape has a smaller band gap. The shape of 2D QDs also affects their magnetism. The hexagonal, triangular, and parallelogram shapes are referred to as nonmagnetic, ferromagnetic, and antiferromagnetic materials for GQDs and SiQDs, respectively. The magnetic moment of triangular GQDs and SiQDs depends on the size of QDs [15,16].

Nanomaterials are known for their easy tuning characteristics, and band tuning plays a crucial role in device applications, known as band engineering. Deforming the material is considered a powerful method for band engineering because it alters the symmetry of the material, which subsequently impacts its electronic structure. For example, the band gap of a graphene nanoribbon with armchair edges can be tuned by uniaxial strains with a zigzag-shaped oscillation [43] and strained 2D transition metal dichalcogenide monolayers show a direct-to-indirect band gap transition [44].

In addition, combined 2D QD and its hydrogenated counterpart systems can be designed as QD arrays. The embedded GQDs array in graphane and the embedded SiQD array in silicane, denoted as GQDs/graphane and SiQDs/silicane, respectively, are composite two-dimensional heterostructures. The inter-dot interaction and the deformation effect are also interesting topics for applications in quantum computing and flexible displays. This paper presents the calculated results of both two-dimensional heterostructure systems (2DHS), GQDs/graphane and SiQDs/silicane, including the inter-dot interaction and deformation effect on the electronic and magnetic properties of the 2D QD array. The physical properties of GQDs and SiQDs affected by strain are also compared.

Graphene, graphane, silicene, and silicane all have a honeycomb structure, and the plane of graphene is flat, while that of the others is bulked and the buckling is enhanced after hydrogenation. For a GQD and SiQD, we named the QDs based on their shape, for example, QDs with a hexagonal shape are referred to as hexagonal QDs and other shaped QDs adhere to the same naming convention. The hexagonal GQD with twenty-four carbon atoms is named Hex-24 GQD, with hydrogen atoms terminating carbon atoms at the edge (Fig. 1 (a)). The Hex-24 SiQD follows the same naming convention. Fig. 1(b) displays the structure of a 4 × 4 Hex-6 GQDs/graphane (SiQDs/silicane), where GQDs (SiQDs) are in the region of C (Si) atoms without hydrogen atoms, and the inter-dot distance (D) is therein. The n×n Hex-N GQDs/graphane (SiQDs/silicane) is referred to as Hex-N GQD/graphene (SiQD/silicane), where N denotes the number of C (Si) atoms inside the GQD (SiQD). The QDs considered in this study all have zigzag edges. The notation of n×n GQDs/graphane (SiQDs/silicane) denotes that the GQD (SiQD) array forms a n×n periodic condition. A smaller value of n corresponds to a denser arrangement of GQD (SiQD) in graphane (silicane).

For the deformation effect, we considered a 2D homogeneous strain and two types of uniaxial strains applied on the 2DHS plane. The homogeneous strain is an equal biaxial strain, and the structure retains its symmetry under such a strain. The homogeneous strain (H-strain
E H
) is defined as
E H
=
a −
a 0
/
a 0
, where a (a 0) is the deformed (undeformed) lattice constant of the QD array system. The uniaxial strain is defined as
E u
=
(
L −
L 0
)
/
L 0
, where L and L 0 correspond to the deformed and undeformed unit lengths in a specific direction. Negative and positive
E H
or
E u
represent compressive and tensile strains, respectively. The strain applied in the zigzag direction is named Z-strain, while the deformation in the armchair direction is called A-strain, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The symmetry of Brillouin zone should be changed for structure under A and Z uniaxial strains and the Brillouin zone also given in Fig. 1(c). It also had been addressed in previous investigations [45,46].

The calculations were performed using a first-principles method based on the density functional theory [47] and the generalized gradient approximation [48]. The Vienna ab initio simulation package was used. The projector augmented-wave method [49,50] was used to determine the potentials, which were in the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof functional form for the carbon, silicon, and hydrogen ions [51]. The wave functions were expanded by plane waves, with a cutoff energy of 500 eV for the GQDs system and 312.5 eV for the SiQDs system. Spin polarization was taken into account, and although spin-orbit coupling was considered, its impact was negligible and could be disregarded in this system. The hybrid functionals HSE06 [52,53] were also included to examine the reliability of the tendency of the band gap variation in the large 2DHSs. The examination is performed in the SiQD 2DHS. The Monkhorst–Pack scheme [54] was adopted for k-point sampling. The k-point mesh was tested up to 15 × 15 × 1, and the k-mesh used depends on the size of the supercell of the 2DHS. One k point was chosen for the single QDs. The system was relaxed until the Hellmann–Feynman force was less than 0.01 eV/Å. The system was simulated using a supercell with periodic boundary conditions in the x–y plane and a vacuum layer in the z-direction. We considered 2 × 2, 3 × 3, 4 × 4, 6 × 6, 8 × 8, and 12 × 12 supercells in the x-y plane, each containing numerous atoms (up to 576 in a single supercell). A vacuum layer with a thickness of 20 Å was included to prevent any artificial atomic interactions between adjacent supercells.

The strain energy, denoted as
E
s t r a i n
(
E H
)
, and the inter-dot interaction energy, represented by
Δ E
( D )
, are defined as follows:
(1)
E
s t r a i n
(
E H
)
= E
(
E H
)
−
E 0
,
(2)
E
d o t
(
n × n
)
= E
(
n × n
)
−
(
2
n 2
− N
)
E
C − H
(
o r
E
S i − H
)
,
(3)
Δ E
( D )
=
E
d o t
(
n × n
)
−
E
d o t
(
12 × 12
)
,
where
E
(
E H
)
and
E 0
are the total energy with and without strain. N is the number of carbon or silicon atoms in a QD.
E
d o t
(
n × n
)
and
E
(
n × n
)
are the dot energy and the total energy of the QD array with n×n periodic structure, respectively. The
E
C − H
, and
E
S i − H
are the total energy of one pair of C–H and Si–H, respectively, which obtained by the total energy of graphane, and silicane. The inter-dot energy
Δ E
( D )
of a QD array with 12 × 12 periodic structure is referred to as the reference.

Firstly, we examine the strain effect for the GQDs/Graphane and SiQDs/Silicane arrays with various periodic conditions from the energy perspective. Fig. 2 (a) and b display the strain energy of hexagonal GQD/Graphane and SiQD/Silicane with six atoms each QD, and four periodic conditions are shown. The energy in an equivalent supercell for different periodic arrays is used. H-, A-, and Z-strain are investigated, but only the E strain of H-strain is presented as the behavior of strain energy under strains is similar, while the E strain under A- and Z-strains is about half of that under H-strain. The E strain-strain curves are asymmetric under compression and tension, with the E strain under compressive strain being larger than that under tensile strain. This asymmetric behavior is similar to that of graphene, graphane, silicene, and silicane (insets in Fig. 2). The QD array with a smaller periodic condition, which has a smaller inter-dot distance, has the larger E strain. This is because fully hydrogenated systems reduce the hardness of the system, and a QD array with a smaller inter-dot distance is related to dense QD and has a larger area without hydrogen adsorption. Fig. S1 presents the E strain of hexagonal and triangular QD with various sizes in a 6 × 6 periodic structure. The E strain curves also exhibit asymmetrically under compressive and tensile strains. For a large-size QD array, the E strain under compressive strains is smaller than that under tensile strains, which is opposite to the E strain of pure graphene, graphane, silicene, and silicane systems. The smaller E strain of a large-size QD array is due to the strain sharing by the hydrogenated counterpart under compressive strains. Interestingly, it can be observed that the actual size of the QD is larger than the size that should be under larger compressive strain. This reveals that the hydrogenated part shares some strain from the portion of C or Si atoms without hydrogen. This asymmetric phenomenon is more significant in the SiQD/Silicane system than in the GQD/Graphane system. The GQD/Graphane system is harder to deform than the SiQD/Silicane system. The strain sharing caused softening can be observed in the
E
S t r a i n
curves of SiQD/Silicane, which is more apparent for the triangular QD array.

The interaction energy
Δ E
, as shown in Fig. 2(c–d) and defined in Eq. (3), indicates that the interaction depends on the shape and the species of QDs. The Tri-4 GQDs in graphane show the strongest interaction among the four cases in Fig. 2(c–d). The
Δ E
of Tri-4 SiQDs in silicane is smaller than that of Tri4-GQDs in graphane, with a value of approximately one-tenth. The
Δ E
of Hex-6 GQDs in graphane becomes much smaller than that between Tri-4 GQD.

The magnetic properties of QDs depend on both their shape and size, with shape being the dominant factor in determining the spin density distribution. According to a theorem for electrons on biparticle lattice, the inequality of the number of atoms sit on A site and B site provide the unparired electron and resulted the magnetic properties of the QD [55]. Triangular SiQDs and GQDs exhibit magnetic alternation with spin up and spin down on the adjacent Si or carbon atoms, while parallelogram SiQDs and GQDs are antiferromagnetic [15,16]. The magnetic properties of both 2DHS can be affected by strains; however, the strain effect on magnetism is much stronger in SiQDs/Silicane than in GQDs/Graphane. Firstly, we examine the Tri-4 SiQDs in silicane, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). Tri-4 SiQD in silicane transforms from a magnetic to a nonmagnetic semiconductor under a compressive H-strain of −7%, except for the 2 × 2 periodic condition. Tri-4 SiQDs/Silicane with a 2 × 2 periodic condition begins to lose its magnetism under −5% H- and Z-strains and −7% A-strain. The magnetism of Tri-4 SiQDs in silicane does not change significantly under tensile strains or compressive A- and Z-strains, except for the 2 × 2 periodic condition. Under a tensile H-strain of 10 %, the magnetic moment of the four Si atoms inside Tri-4 SiQD with a 2 × 2 periodic condition in silicane decreases by approximately 0.1
μ B
. It indicates that the strain sharing accompanying the interdot interaction alters the magnetic properties of a SiQD in silicane. Fig. 3(b) displays the total magnetic moments (
μ
t o t
) of the four triangular SiQD arrays with different QD sizes in the 6 × 6 periodic condition. The strain sharing under the H-strain is stronger than under the other two uniaxial strains (A- and Z-strains), which caused a stronger variation of the magnet moment. Tri-16 and Tri-36 SiQDs in silicane lose their magnetic moment under compressive strain around −5 %. However, the Tri-25 SiQD firstly converts from a magnetic to a nonmagnetic semiconductor under an H-strain of around −3%, and the transformation to nonmagnetic is slower under compressive A- and Z-strains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strain effect on electronics of GQDs/Graphane
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 presents the energy gap (E g) of Hex-6 and Tri-4 GQD/Graphane with various periodic conditions, along with the HOMO-LUMO gap of single Hex-6 and Tri-4 GQDs, and the band gap of graphene for comparison. As the strain increases, the E g of Hex-6 GQD arrays exhibits an increase, contrary to the behavior of single Hex-6 GQD. This is because the HOMO and LUMO of Hex-6 GQD mix with the valence and conduction bands of graphane, respectively. The state and band mixing can be observed in the band structures (Fig. S2) and the partial density of states (PDOS) (Fig. S3) of Hex6-QD arrays under strains. Furthermore, the HOMO and LUMO of a single Hex-6 GQD and the valence band maximum (VBM) and conduction band minimum (CBM) of the GQDs/Graphane system and graphane are drawn in Figs. S4(a–c) for detailed understanding. The CBM of the Hex-6 GQD array is dominated by graphane; on the contrary, the VBM of the GQD array is determined by the Hex-6 GQD. In response to tensile strain, the conduction band of graphane rises and the LUMO of Hex-6 GQD becomes the lowest conduction state (see Fig. S2). Consequently, the rate of increase in E g is moderated, as drawn in Fig. 4(a). Similarly, under tensile A-strain and Z-strains, mirroring the band structure response observed under H-strains, the LUMO of Hex-6 GQD descends to become the CBM of the 2DHS, leading to a gradual decrease in E g (see Fig. 4(b–c)).

It can be observed in Fig. 4 that the E
g of Tri-4 GQDs is smaller than that of Hex-6 GQDs and is less sensitive to strains. The strain insensitivity of E g in Tri-4 GQD arrays resembles that of a single Tri-4 GQD, but it shows a slightly more reactive response to strains than its individual counterpart. Among the four Tri-4 GQD arrays, the 3 × 3 Tri-4 array is the most responsive to strain. The E g of the 3 × 3 Tri-4 GQD array increases with strain, exhibiting variations of approximately 0.80, 0.48, and 0.54 eV under H-strain, A-strain, and Z-strain, ranging from −7% to 10 %, respectively.

Fig. 5 displays the E g of various sizes and shapes of GQDs in 6 × 6 periodic arrays, as well as that of single GQDs with hexagonal, triangular, and parallelogram shapes for comparison. As the energy gap of QDs depends on their shape, the figure illustrates that the single hexagonal GQD and hexagonal GQD 2DHS have a higher energy gap than that of the triangular and parallelogram ones. Additionally, the GQD array with a larger dot size has a smaller E g. The single hexagonal GQD (Hex24, Tri-36, and Par-72) all have a larger energy gap than that of the GQD array. The E g of both single hexagonal GQD and GQD arrays consistently decrease with increasing H-strain, except the Hex-54 GQD array, which exhibits a deviation under H-strain ranging from −7% to −5%. On the other hand, under A- and Z-strains, the E g-strain curves exhibit an Λ shape for both individual hexagonal GQD and GQD arrays. Fig. 6 (a) shows the band structure of Hex-24 GQD/Graphane systems. As the HOMO and LUMO of Hex-24 and Hex-54 GQD arrays reside between the CBM and VBM of the graphane counterpart, the energy in the low energy region is dominated by the GQD part rather than the graphane counterpart in the GQD/Graphane 2DHS. The strain-E g curves of Hex-24 and Hex-54 GQD arrays have similar behavior to that of a single Hex-24 GQD. The difference between the largest to smallest E g of Hex-24 (
Δ
E g
) are 0.44, 0.56, and 0.55 eV under H-, A-, and Z-strain, respectively. These values are smaller than the corresponding values of 1.22, 0.78, and 0.70 eV for a single Hex-24 GQD under H-, A-, and Z-strain, respectively. This is due to the strain on the GQD in the GQD/Graphane system, which is partly shared by the graphane component.

The energy gap of Tri-36 GQD arrays and the single Tri-36 GQD shows different responses to strains, due to the heterostructure of the region with and without adsorbed hydrogen and the strain sharing effect. The energy gap of Tri-36 GQD array under H-strain exhibits a monotonic increase, with a
Δ
E g
(0.24 eV), surpassing the values (around 0.1 eV) observed under A- and Z-strains. The energy gap of Tri-36 and Par-72 arrays is dominated by the GQD part, given that their HOMO and LUMO reside within the band gap region of graphane, as demonstrated in the band structure of Tri-36 GQD array shown in Fig. 6(b). The graphane part has less influence on the electronic structures in the low energy region of the triangular and parallelogram 2DHS. The Tri-36 GQD array exhibits as a magnetic semiconductor, wherein the spin orientation of valence and conduction states in the low energy region are opposite. The magnetism of the Tri-36 GQD array is retained even under strains ranging from −7% to 10 %. The valence and conduction states are represented by flat bands in the band structure of 2DHS. For the Tri-36 GQD array, the valence states in the low energy region appear slightly broadened under large compressive and tensile strains, while conduction states become broader and shift upward with increasing H-strain increases. The difference between the
Δ
E g
of Par-72 GQD array and single GQD are smaller than those observed for the Tri-36 and Hex-24 2DHS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strain effect on electronics of SiQDs/Silicane
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 depicts the energy gap of three shapes of SiQDs/Silicane 2DHS under various periodic conditions and three types of strains. The HSE06 funtionals are used to calculate Hex-24 SiQD array in 4 × 4 periodic condition and Tri-4 SiQD array in 2 × 2 and 4 × 4 periodic conditions. The variation of band gaps is in Fig. S5 and the band structure of Hex-24 SiQD array and Tri-4 SiQD array under H-strain are shown in Figs. S6 and S7. The results reveal the PBE calculation on the variation tendency of the band gap is reliable. Since the Brillouin zone are changed under A-, and Z-uniaxial strains, the band structure of Hex-6 SiQD array in 2 × 2 periodic condition is also presented in Fig. S8 to examine the influence of the changes of the Brillouin zone to the band gap. As the CBM and VBM of silicane both locate on the zone center, and the states of QD are in the low energy region, the distortion of the Brillouin zone does not affect the band gap in different high symmetry direction. The energy gap curves of single SiQDs in the three shapes (Hex-6, Tri4-and Par-8) are included, along with that of silicane, for comparison. The E g of Hex-6 and Tri-4 SiQD arrays exhibit distinct strain behavior compared to their single SiQDs and silicane. The E g curve of silicane displays like Λ shape under H, A-, and Z-strains, with the maximum located at 1 % for H-stain but 0 % for A- and Z-strains. While the E g curve of a single Hex-6 SiQD consistently exhibits a monotonic decline with increasing H-strain, it takes on a Λ shape under the A-, and Z-strain conditions. Notably, the slope of the left leg of Λ is approximately one-fifth and one-seventh of that on the right leg under the A- and Z-strain, respectively. The E g curve of a single Tri-4 SiQD shows a gradual increase with strain, albeit in a small amount of less than 0.1 eV. However, in SiQD 2DHS, the E g response to strains forms a distinctive hat-shaped curve, initially increasing with strain, transitioning into a region of gradual growth, reaching its maximum, and ultimately descending. The maximum value of E g in Hex-6 SiQD arrays is achieved at approximately 3 % H-strain for 2 × 2 and 4 × 4 periodic conditions, and at around 2 % for 3 × 3 and 6 × 6 SiQD arrays, but the maximum E g of Hex-6 SiQD arrays occurs at zero A- and Z-strains. The steeply inclined region is observed within the strain range of less than −3% or greater than 3 %. Figs. S4(d–f) illustrates the VBM and CBM of silicane and 2DHS, along with the HOMO and LUMO of a single SiQD, under strains for further examination. The VBM of silicane is slightly higher than the HOMO of a single Hex-6 SiQD, while the CBM of silicane overlaps with the LUMO of the single SiQD. As the value of strains grows, the CBM of silicane undergoes a downward, shift, ultimately surpassing the LUMO of SiQD under both compressive and tensile strains. The VBM (CBM) of the SiQD 2DHS is higher (lower) than that of the two individual parts, indicating that the interaction is much stronger between silicane and SiQD in the 2DHS. Fig. S9(a) shows the band structures of the Hex-6 2DHS under H-strains with a 6 × 6 periodic condition. Surprisingly, the states belonging to the SiQD part appear in the band gap, and the LUMO of Hex-6 SiQD ascends as the H-strain increases from −7% to 2 %, then mixes into the conduction band. The HOMO of Hex-6 SiQD also moves upward as the tensile H-strain increases. Both the VBM and CBM of the silicane part in the Hex-6 2DHS decrease as the strain increases to 5 %, then the CBM continuously descends and the VBM rises as the strain continuously increases. Nonetheless, the HOMO and LUMO of Hex-6 SiQD part ascend with increasing strain. This phenomenon is attributed to the contrasting behavior of the band edges in the SiQD and silicane parts in response to strain, resulting in a lower band gap in the region with higher compressive and tensile strain. Additionally, the silicane part in the SiQD 2DHS shares some of the strains with its counterpart without hydrogenation, contributing to the variation in the strain-E g curve. However, the silicane part becomes over-stressed, especially in the large strain region, causing the VBM to move upward and the CBM to move downward significantly under high compressive and tensile strain, respectively.

While the HOMO-LUMO gap of a single Tri-4 SiQD is insensitive to strains, the strain-E g curves of Tri4 SiQD arrays exhibit a hat shape, mirroring the tendency in the Hex-6 SiQD array (see Fig. 7(a–c)). However, the mechanism of the Tri-4 and Hex-6 2DHS is not the same. The energy gap of Tri-4 SiQD arrays increases slowly under the strains ranging from −3% to 5 %. The slow-varying character is similar to that of a single Tri-4 SiQD under strain, as the HOMO and LUMO of Tri-4 SiQD are located within the band gap of silicane, as can be observed in the band structure of Tri-4 SiQD array (Fig. S9(b)). The HOMO and LUMO of Tri-4 SiQD have opposite spin orientations and are not sensitive to strain in the range of −3%–5 %. As the H-strain (A- and Z-strain) is greater than 5 % (7 %), the LUMO of Tri-4 SiQD mixed with silicane, and the CBM of silicane descends while the HOMO of SiQD ascends. Furthermore, the CBM becomes lower than the Fermi level, and the energy gap becomes zero at 10 % H-strain. For the compressive configuration, when the strain is less than −2%, the VBM is continuously raised and exceeds the HOMO of SiQD. Additionally, the VBM of silicane breakthroughs the Fermi level when the H-strain reaches −7%. The Tri-4 SiQD array transfers from a magnetic semiconductor to a nonmagnetic metal at −5% H-, and Z-strains, and −7% A-strain for 2 × 2 periodic condition (Fig. 7(a–c) and Fig. 3(a)), but the transformation is delayed until H-strain is at −7% for other periodic conditions.

While the single Par-8 SiQD is antiferromagnetic, the Par-8 SiQD array is nonmagnetic. The antiferromagnetic property of the single Par-8 SiQD can be converted to nonmagnetic under both compressive and tensile strains, with the conversion occurring when the H-strain reaches −7% or exceeds 5 %, under 10 % A-strain, and Z-strain less than −3%. The energy gap of a single Par-8 SiQD has a slow variation in the H-strain range of −3%–5 %, and the E g decreases from 1.00 eV to 0.59 eV as the H-strain increases from 5 % to 10 %. The variation of E g is minimal for the QD under A- and Z-strains. As shown in Fig. S9(c), the Par-8 SiQD array mixes the energy level of SiQD and the band of silicane. Both the conduction and valence band of the silicane part in the SiQD 2DHS descends with increasing H-strain. The conduction band of silicane in the SiQD 2DHS descends under the compressive A-strain and remains nearly the same height under the compressive Z-strain. The strong mixing of bands in silicane and energy levels of SiQD in the Par-8 SiQD array leads to the closure of the energy gap at 10 % H-strain.

Fig. 8 shows the variations in the energy gap response to strains for three different shapes of SiQD arrays, along with the strain-E g curves of a single SiQD for comparison. The strain-E g curves of SiQD arrays exhibit a slow-varying region (SVR), which depends on the dot shape, size of the SiQD array, and the type of strain applied. The SVR is smaller under the H-strain than under the A- and Z-strains for all three types of SiQD arrays (i.e., hexagonal, triangular, and parallelogram).

For the Hex-24 SiQD 6 × 6 array, the SVR occurs within the ranges from −3% to 3 %, −5%–7 %, and −5%–5 % under H-, A-, and Z-strain, respectively. For the Hex-54 SiQD 6 × 6 array, the SVR ranges from −5% to 5 %, −7%–5 %, and −5%–7 % under H-, A-, and Z-strain, respectively. The variation in the E g of a single SiQD differs from that of a SiQD array. In a SiQD/Silicane 2DHS, the silicane part is subject to more strain than the SiQD part, resulting in a smaller variation of the energy level of SiQD but a larger influence on the band structure of the silicane part. The HOMO and LUMO of Hex-24 and Hex-54 SiQD reside in the band gap of silicane. The HOMO-LUMO gap of SiQD is less sensitive to overall strains and exhibits a SVR in the strain- E g curves due to the smaller strain sharing of SiQD compared to the silicane part. Outside the SVR, the band structure of silicane strongly mixes with energy levels of SiQDs, leading to significant decay of the energy gap as the compressive and tensile strains increase. Fig. 9 (a) shows the band structure of the Hex-24 SiQD 6 × 6 array under the H-strain. Outside the SVR, the energy levels of Hex-24 SiQD strongly mix with the bands of silicane under −5% H-strain. Simultaneously, the conduction bands of silicane decrease continuously, and the energy levels of SiQD increase. This leads to a metallic-like behavior as the H-strain approaches 10 %.

The strain-sharing effect is more significant in a 2DHS with larger SiQDs than those with smaller SiQDs. In the case of a Hex-54 SiQD array, it exhibits metallic-like behavior under a 7 % H-strain. Similarly, Tri-36 also becomes metallic-like at 7 % H-strain, while smaller triangular SiQD arrays require 10 % H-strain to become metallic-like. However, the magnetism of triangular SiQD arrays persists under tensile H-strains, as mentioned previously. Interestingly, compressive H-strains not only reduce the magnetism but also convert the SiQD from a semiconductor to a metal, specifically, Tri-4 and Tri-25 require −7% and −3% H-strain, respectively, and other triangular SiQD arrays require −5% H-strain. The band structure of the Tri-36 SiQD 6 × 6 array is presented in Fig. 9(b), indicating the phase transition of a ferromagnetic semiconductor to a nonmagnetic metal at −5% H-strain and the transformation of a ferromagnetic semiconductor to a metal under H-tensile strain near 7 %.

Single parallelogram SiQDs exhibit antiferromagnetic behavior, which remains unchanged even under strains for single Par-32 and Par-72 SiQDs. The magnetic behavior of parallelogram SiQD arrays closely mirrors that of the single SiQD. However, under higher compressive strains, parallelogram 2DHS can be transformed from an antiferromagnetic to a nonmagnetic semiconductor. Additionally, the parallelogram SiQD arrays undergo a transition from an antiferromagnetic semiconductor to an antiferromagnetic metal under a 10 % tensile H-strain. The former is attributed to the interaction between SiQDs associated with the strain sharing under compressive strains, while the latter is from strain sharing between the silicane part and the mixing of energy levels of SiQDs and bands of silicane, as illustrated in Fig. 9(c).

We conducted a series of DFT calculations to examine the deformation effects on the 2DHS of GQD/Graphane and SiQD/Silicane. The band gap can be tuned from several meV to 1.20 eV (1.35 eV) by different strain types, dot sizes, and shapes in GQD 2DHS (SiQD 2DHS). It provides opportunities for applications in electronic and optical devices. In addition to the regular strain effect, the manipulation of the electronic properties is also contributed by the interdot interaction and the strain-sharing effect for graphane or silicane. The interdot interaction is stronger for the GQD/Graphane than the SiQD/Silicane, whereas the strain-sharing effect is notably more significant in SiQD/Silicane than in GQD/Graphane. The strain-sharing effect induces a downward shift in the conduction band of silicane, leading to the transition from a semiconductor to a metal under higher tensile strains. The strain sharing, accompanied by interdot interaction, results in the magnetic-to-nonmagnetic transition for triangular and parallelogram SiQD arrays and the semiconductor-to-metal transition for hexagonal and triangular SiQD arrays. The magnetic properties of GQD/Graphane do not change under deformations. The strain effect can be used to tune the magnetic and nonmagnetic properties of the triangular and parallelogram SiQD/Silicane, which can be applied in nanoscale registrations of digital information. SiQDs/Silicane can also be utilized to design silicon-based microelectronic devices.
This work was supported by Cheng Gung University under grant numbers UMRPD5M001.

Bi-Ru Wu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Cheng Gung University s under grant number UMRPD5M001.
